articles.title: The World Cup vs. bugs, germs and heat: Here's the game plan
articles.summary: Despite a diminished federal presence, public health departments are preparing for common ailments that could afflict fans who gather for the event — and are keeping an eye on the Ebola outbreak, too.
articles.titleZh: 世界杯对抗虫子、细菌和高温：这是比赛计划
articles.summaryZh: 尽管联邦政府的存在有所减少，但公共卫生部门正在为可能困扰聚集在现场的球迷的常见疾病做好准备，并且也在密切关注埃博拉疫情的爆发。

contentParagraphs.en: ATLANTA, GEORGIA - MAY 16: Attendees stand in line for the fan mart during the FIFA World Cup Trophy Tour on May 16, 2026 in Atlanta, Georgia.
contentParagraphs.zh: 佐治亚州亚特兰大 - 5 月 16 日：2026 年 5 月 16 日在佐治亚州亚特兰大举行的国际足联世界杯奖杯巡演期间，与会者在球迷市场排队。

contentParagraphs.en: Todd Kirkland/FIFA/via Getty Images
contentParagraphs.zh: 托德柯克兰/国际足联/来自盖蒂图片社

contentParagraphs.en: Todd Kirkland/FIFA/via Getty Images
contentParagraphs.zh: 托德柯克兰/国际足联/来自盖蒂图片社

contentParagraphs.en: Millions of people are about to pack stadiums across the U.S., Canada and Mexico for the World Cup, starting June 11.
contentParagraphs.zh: 从 6 月 11 日开始，数百万人将挤满美国、加拿大和墨西哥的体育场观看世界杯。

contentParagraphs.en: Public health officials have been working to ensure the health and safety of the masses of fans they're expecting at the 104 matches across 16 cities through mid-July.
contentParagraphs.zh: 截至 7 月中旬，公共卫生官员一直在努力确保广大球迷的健康和安全，他们将在 16 个城市观看 104 场比赛。

contentParagraphs.en: In the U.S., World Cup preparations are coming in a time of a diminished federal health presence and funding. It's upped the workload for host city health departments, as they're also strapped for federal funding.
contentParagraphs.zh: 在美国，世界杯的筹备工作正值联邦卫生部门的存在和资金减少之际。这增加了主办城市卫生部门的工作量，因为他们也面临联邦资金的短缺。

contentParagraphs.en: Nevertheless, World Cup host city health officials say they're prepared for the many challenges that come with large-scale celebrations and gatherings.
contentParagraphs.zh: 尽管如此，世界杯主办城市的卫生官员表示，他们已做好应对大规模庆祝活动和集会带来的许多挑战的准备。

contentParagraphs.en: "We've been focusing on expecting the expected" — issues related to weather, health and human behavior, says Dr. Marcus Plescia, district health director for Fulton County, Georgia, which includes Atlanta.
contentParagraphs.zh: 佐治亚州富尔顿县（包括亚特兰大）的地区卫生主任马库斯·普莱西亚博士说，“我们一直专注于期待预期的结果”——与天气、健康和人类行为有关的问题。

contentParagraphs.en: Planning for the event is ongoing, says Dr. Philip Huang, director of Dallas County Health and Human Services in Texas. "To be able to begin preparations a year out is a luxury for us, compared to a lot of the situations that we deal with," he says.
contentParagraphs.zh: 德克萨斯州达拉斯县卫生与公众服务部主任 Philip Huang 博士表示，该活动的规划正在进行中。 “与我们处理的许多情况相比，能够在一年后开始准备对我们来说是一种奢侈，”他说。

contentParagraphs.en: "We call Atlanta 'Hotlanta' for a reason"
contentParagraphs.zh: “我们称亚特兰大为‘Hotlanta’是有原因的”

contentParagraphs.en: For public health departments, the World Cup is a big deal –- but they're well prepared for the biggest challenges.
contentParagraphs.zh: 对于公共卫生部门来说，世界杯是一件大事，但他们已经做好了应对最大挑战的准备。

contentParagraphs.en: Summer heat, for instance. "We call Atlanta 'Hotlanta' for a reason," says Georgia's Dr. Plescia, "It's going to be hot and humid here -– so heat-related injuries are going to be an issue."
contentParagraphs.zh: 以夏季炎热为例。 “我们称亚特兰大为‘Hotlanta’是有原因的，”佐治亚州的普莱西亚博士说，“这里会变得又热又湿，所以与热有关的伤害将成为一个问题。”

contentParagraphs.en: Atlanta is hosting eight World Cup matches and the team from Uzbekistan. At downtown Centennial Olympic Park, a month-long fan festival is expected to draw some 15,000 people a day. Cooling stations will offer air-conditioning and hydration.
contentParagraphs.zh: 亚特兰大将主办八场世界杯比赛，参赛球队为乌兹别克斯坦队。在市中心的百年奥林匹克公园，为期一个月的粉丝节预计每天将吸引约 15,000 人。冷却站将提供空调和水化服务。

contentParagraphs.en: Then there's feeding the crowds. "The biggest lift for us and for all health departments is that our environmental health workers have to inspect all of the food vendors –- and there will be a whole lot of food vendors," Plescia says.
contentParagraphs.zh: 然后就是喂饱人群。 “对我们和所有卫生部门来说，最大的提升是我们的环境卫生工作人员必须检查所有食品摊贩——而且将会有很多食品摊贩，”普莱斯西亚说。

contentParagraphs.en: Inspectors will check every day to ensure the facilities are in working order and the food is safe to eat. "We want to make sure that people feel confident that they can go out to eat and have a good time –- and they don't have to worry about getting food poisoning," Plescia says.
contentParagraphs.zh: 检查员每天都会进行检查，以确保设施正常运转并且食品可以安全食用。 “我们希望确保人们有信心可以出去吃饭并度过愉快的时光，而且不必担心食物中毒，”普莱斯西亚说。

contentParagraphs.en: When lots of people gather, germs tend to spread. "We're worried about sexually transmitted infections because there's going to be a lot of people here and it's going to be a festive atmosphere," Plescia says. "And then we're worried about things maybe we wouldn't have been worried about as much before, particularly measles." Measles is highly contagious among those who haven't been vaccinated, and there have been 30 U.S. outbreaks this year.
contentParagraphs.zh: 当很多人聚集时，细菌很容易传播。 “我们担心性传播感染，因为这里会有很多人，而且会有节日气氛，”普莱斯西亚说。 “然后我们担心一些以前可能不会担心的事情，特别是麻疹。”麻疹在未接种疫苗的人中具有高度传染性，今年美国已爆发 30 起麻疹疫情。

contentParagraphs.en: Dallas is hosting nine World Cup matches — the most of any city. The health department is stepping up disease surveillance in wastewater, expanding sites and covering more area, says Dr. Huang, the county health authority. Beyond looking for typical threats like influenza and COVID, they're also testing for "whatever might appear" through genomic sequencing, he says.
contentParagraphs.zh: 达拉斯将举办九场世界杯比赛，是所有城市中举办次数最多的。县卫生局局长黄博士表示，卫生部门正在加强废水疾病监测，扩大监测点并覆盖更多区域。他说，除了寻找流感和新冠病毒等典型威胁之外，他们还通过基因组测序来测试“可能出现的任何情况”。

contentParagraphs.en: They're also broadening mosquito surveillance –- setting insect traps and testing for pathogens they can spread. "We always test for West Nile virus," Huang says, "But we're also increasing our testing for dengue, chikungunya and Zika because of all the [incoming] international travel."
contentParagraphs.zh: 他们还扩大了蚊子监测范围——设置昆虫陷阱并测试它们可以传播的病原体。 “我们总是检测西尼罗河病毒，”黄说，“但由于所有[入境]国际旅行，我们也增加了对登革热、基孔肯雅热和寨卡病毒的检测。”

contentParagraphs.en: Medical staff are also looking out for symptoms of Ebola or hantavirus, due to outbreaks.
contentParagraphs.zh: 由于疫情爆发，医务人员也在寻找埃博拉病毒或汉坦病毒的症状。

contentParagraphs.en: Keeping track of which diseases are circulating means they'll be able to alert hospitals and the public quickly if they see a spike. "It's a lot of things we normally do but certainly more enhanced," Huang says.
contentParagraphs.zh: 跟踪哪些疾病正在传播意味着他们能够在发现病例激增时迅速向医院和公众发出警报。黄说：“这是我们通常做的很多事情，但肯定会更加加强。”

contentParagraphs.en: They're also preparing health tips for the public in different languages: Wear a hat and sunscreen and hydrate when it's hot out. Bug spray and long sleeves help prevent mosquito bites. Stay home if you're sick. "It's the same messages [we always share], but it's really concentrated because we've got so many people coming during a hot period of time in Dallas," Huang says.
contentParagraphs.zh: 他们还用不同的语言为公众准备健康提示：天气炎热时戴帽子、涂防晒霜并补充水分。杀虫剂和长袖有助于防止蚊虫叮咬。如果你生病了，请呆在家里。 “[我们总是分享]同样的信息，但信息非常集中，因为在达拉斯炎热的时期有很多人来，”黄说。

contentParagraphs.en: "Most of our staff are not taking vacations"
contentParagraphs.zh: “我们大多数员工都没有休假”

contentParagraphs.en: The World Cup finals are jointly hosted by New York and New Jersey on July 19.
contentParagraphs.zh: 世界杯决赛将于7月19日由纽约和新泽西联合举办。

contentParagraphs.en: Officials have been running practice simulations to prepare for outbreaks and mass casualty events. "We plan for the worst case scenario and go through it before it actually happens," says Dr. Alister Martin, New York City's health commissioner.
contentParagraphs.zh: 官员们一直在进行模拟练习，为疫情爆发和大规模伤亡事件做好准备。纽约市卫生专员阿利斯特·马丁博士说：“我们为最坏的情况做好了计划，并在它真正发生之前进行了应对。”

contentParagraphs.en: They've also set up "incident command" to "divert resources from normal stuff so that we can manage any potential emergencies," Martin says, adding that the operation will continue through much of the summer, for other big events planned in New York, including America's 250th anniversary, Pride month and the Puerto Rican Day parade.
contentParagraphs.zh: 马丁说，他们还设立了“事件指挥部”，以“将资源从正常事务中转移出来，以便我们能够应对任何潜在的紧急情况”。他补充说，这一行动将持续整个夏天的大部分时间，用于计划在纽约举行的其他大型活动，包括美国建国 250 周年纪念日、骄傲月和波多黎各日游行。

contentParagraphs.en: In Los Angeles, which is hosting eight games, including the U.S. opening match versus Paraguay, risk-assessment teams will monitor the surroundings for chemical exposures and biological threats.
contentParagraphs.zh: 在洛杉矶，将举办八场比赛，包括美国队对阵巴拉圭队的揭幕战，风险评估小组将监测周围环境是否存在化学暴露和生物威胁。

contentParagraphs.en: In L.A., diminished federal assistance means the health department is counting on existing staff to do the extra work. "Most of our staff are not taking vacations during the month of the World Cup because they'll need to work -– we'll be obviously accruing overtime costs," says Barbara Ferrer, the L.A. county health director.
contentParagraphs.zh: 在洛杉矶，联邦援助的减少意味着卫生部门只能依靠现有的工作人员来完成额外的工作。洛杉矶县卫生总监芭芭拉·费雷尔 (Barbara Ferrer) 表示：“我们的大多数员工在世界杯期间都不会休假，因为他们需要工作——我们显然会增加加班费用。”

contentParagraphs.en: And much of their regular work, like routine food inspections, gets put aside. "I know it would be easier if we weren't in a resource constrained environment," Ferrer says. "But I want to provide assurance that even in a resource constrained environment, we're well prepared."
contentParagraphs.zh: 他们的大部分日常工作，比如例行食品检查，都被搁置了。 “我知道，如果我们不在资源有限的环境中，事情会更容易，”费雷尔说。 “但我想保证，即使在资源有限的环境中，我们也做好了充分准备。”

contentParagraphs.en: Ferrer says they've successfully planned and managed other events of national significance, like the 2022 Super Bowl. "This work isn't new to us," she says, adding that staff are making great efforts "to make sure this is a
contentParagraphs.zh: 费雷尔表示，他们已经成功策划和管理了其他具有全国意义的活动，例如 2022 年超级碗。 “这项工作对我们来说并不新鲜，”她说，并补充说，工作人员正在付出巨大努力“以确保这是一项

articles.title: Scientists are teaching AI-powered robots to run lab experiments
articles.summary: Scientists are building autonomous robotic labs powered by artificial intelligence. The goal, they say, is for these robots to take over human researchers' most laborious, time-consuming tasks.
articles.titleZh: 科学家正在教人工智能驱动的机器人进行实验室实验
articles.summaryZh: 科学家们正在建造由人工智能驱动的自主机器人实验室。他们说，目标是让这些机器人接管人类研究人员最费力、最耗时的任务。

contentParagraphs.en: Reshma Shetty, co-founder and COO of Ginkgo Bioworks, walks through an autonomous lab where AI robots replace lab benches. Shetty says using AI has already fundamentally changed the way she practices science. "The really wild moment was the first time I saw a lab notebook entry written by the model," she says.
contentParagraphs.zh: Ginkgo Bioworks 联合创始人兼首席运营官 Reshma Shetty 走过一个自动化实验室，其中人工智能机器人取代了实验室长凳。谢蒂说，人工智能的使用已经从根本上改变了她实践科学的方式。 “真正疯狂的时刻是我第一次看到模特写的实验室笔记本条目，”她说。

contentParagraphs.en: Jodi Hilton for NPR
contentParagraphs.zh: 乔迪·希尔顿 NPR

contentParagraphs.en: Jodi Hilton for NPR
contentParagraphs.zh: 乔迪·希尔顿 NPR

contentParagraphs.en: Nearly two decades ago, four graduate students from MIT united around a shared idea. "We believed that programming cells would ultimately be more important than programming computers," says Jason Kelly.
contentParagraphs.zh: 大约二十年前，麻省理工学院的四名研究生围绕一个共同的想法团结在一起。 “我们相信对细胞进行编程最终将比对计算机进行编程更重要，”杰森·凯利说。

contentParagraphs.en: Want the latest stories on the science of healthy living? Subscribe to NPR's Health newsletter.
contentParagraphs.zh: 想了解健康生活科学的最新故事吗？订阅 NPR 的健康通讯。

contentParagraphs.en: It felt like an outlandish bet at the time. Things like gene editing or testing new molecules typically demanded many hours in the laboratory — carefully mixing hundreds of chemical cocktails by hand and pipetting them into petri dishes, tasks that required an enormous amount of human labor.
contentParagraphs.zh: 当时感觉这是一个奇怪的赌注。基因编辑或测试新分子之类的事情通常需要在实验室里花费很多时间——手工小心地混合数百种化学混合物并将它们移入培养皿中，这些任务需要大量的人力。

contentParagraphs.en: The first step, they figured, was to speed that process up. So they started a company to replace those human lab workers with robots.
contentParagraphs.zh: 他们认为，第一步是加快这一进程。因此，他们创办了一家公司，用机器人取代实验室的人类工作人员。

contentParagraphs.en: Early potential investors, Kelly recalls, were not excited.
contentParagraphs.zh: 凯利回忆道，早期的潜在投资者并不兴奋。

contentParagraphs.en: " We were living on ramen, buying equipment on eBay, and we could not raise venture capital," he says of their early days running their startup.
contentParagraphs.zh: “我们靠拉面为生，在 eBay 上购买设备，我们无法筹集风险投资，”他在谈到他们创业初期时说道。

contentParagraphs.en: Then came the artificial intelligence boom. In 2014, Kelly remembers reading a blog post from Sam Altman, roughly a year before he went on to found OpenAI. Kelly recalls that Atlman wrote about the potential to automate biotechnology the same way he imagined automating other kinds of technology. The two started talking.
contentParagraphs.zh: 随后出现了人工智能热潮。 2014 年，Kelly 记得读过 Sam Altman 的一篇博文，大约一年后，他创立了 OpenAI。凯利回忆说，阿特曼写过生物技术自动化的潜力，就像他想象的其他类型技术的自动化一样。两人开始交谈。

contentParagraphs.en: " I was like, man, thanks for this blog post," Kelly recalls. "We've been around for five years. It is impossible to raise money."
contentParagraphs.zh: “我当时想，伙计，谢谢你这篇博文，”凯利回忆道。 “我们已经存在五年了。筹集资金是不可能的。”

contentParagraphs.en: Eventually, the Silicon Valley money started flowing.
contentParagraphs.zh: 最终，硅谷的资金开始流动。

contentParagraphs.en: Shetty, left, and Jason Kelly are two of the four co-founders of Ginkgo Bioworks. The group met at MIT, where they hatched an idea to build an automated biotechnology lab. Kelly says it wasn't a popular idea before the AI revolution. "We were living on ramen."
contentParagraphs.zh: 谢蒂（左）和贾森·凯利是 Ginkgo Bioworks 的四位联合创始人中的两位。该小组在麻省理工学院会面，在那里他们萌生了建立一个自动化生物技术实验室的想法。凯利说，在人工智能革命之前，这并不是一个流行的想法。 “我们靠拉面为生。”

contentParagraphs.en: Jodi Hilton for NPR
contentParagraphs.zh: 乔迪·希尔顿 NPR

contentParagraphs.en: Jodi Hilton for NPR
contentParagraphs.zh: 乔迪·希尔顿 NPR

contentParagraphs.en: Today Kelly runs a company, Ginkgo Bioworks, with his former classmates. It has an autonomous laboratory housed in a building overlooking the Boston harbor. Using robotics and AI, Kelly and his co-founders say that they are building the science labs of the future — where human scientists oversee robotic versions of themselves.
contentParagraphs.zh: 如今，凯利与他以前的同学一起经营一家公司，Ginkgo Bioworks。它在俯瞰波士顿港的建筑内设有一个自主实验室。凯利和他的联合创始人表示，他们正在利用机器人技术和人工智能建造未来的科学实验室——人类科学家可以在那里监督自己的机器人版本。

contentParagraphs.en: Pipetting robots
contentParagraphs.zh: 移液机器人

contentParagraphs.en: " Pipetting robots," Kelly says while giving a tour. "I'll show you where we do that."
contentParagraphs.zh: “移液机器人，”凯利在参观时说道。 “我会告诉你我们在哪里做到这一点。”

contentParagraphs.en: Robots are arrayed around the lab, each working on separate science projects. They look nothing like humans — more like one-armed machines, each encased in glass like museum displays. A big screen at the front of the room shows a color-coded schedule of the experiments and each robot's tasks for the day. Below it a track resembling an oversized toy train set runs through the room, delivering equipment from one robot to another.
contentParagraphs.zh: 机器人排列在实验室周围，每个机器人都从事不同的科学项目。它们看起来一点也不像人类——更像是单臂机器，每台机器都像博物馆的展品一样被包裹在玻璃中。房间前面的一个大屏幕显示了用颜色编码的实验时间表和每个机器人当天的任务。在它的下面，一条类似超大玩具火车的轨道穿过房间，将设备从一个机器人运送到另一个机器人。

contentParagraphs.en: Gingko Bioworks does all kinds of work here including pharmaceutical, agricultural and government contracts. Current projects include engineering microbes for better fertilizer and creating proteins that will make snow or ice. They do a significant amount of research on pharmaceuticals.
contentParagraphs.zh: Gingko Bioworks 在这里从事各种工作，包括制药、农业和政府合同。目前的项目包括改造微生物以获得更好的肥料，以及制造能够制造雪或冰的蛋白质。他们对药物进行了大量研究。

contentParagraphs.en: The autonomous lab works on a range of pharmaceutical , agricultural, government and other projects. One current assignment includes engineering microbes for better fertilizer and creating proteins that will make snow or ice.
contentParagraphs.zh: 该自主实验室致力于一系列制药、农业、政府和其他项目。目前的一项任务包括改造微生物以获得更好的肥料，以及制造能够制造雪或冰的蛋白质。

contentParagraphs.en: Jodi Hilton for NPR
contentParagraphs.zh: 乔迪·希尔顿 NPR

contentParagraphs.en: Jodi Hilton for NPR
contentParagraphs.zh: 乔迪·希尔顿 NPR

contentParagraphs.en: "That one there," says Kelly, gesturing to a petri dish being ferried from one robot to another, "that has actual live cells in it."
contentParagraphs.zh: “那里的那个，”凯利指着一个从一个机器人转移到另一个机器人的培养皿说，“里面有真正的活细胞。”

contentParagraphs.en: To do this work, scientists use AI to translate experimental designs into instructions for robots about the work they need done in the lab.
contentParagraphs.zh: 为了完成这项工作，科学家使用人工智能将实验设计转化为机器人在实验室中需要完成的工作的指令。

contentParagraphs.en: Empowering the robots to be the scientists
contentParagraphs.zh: 让机器人成为科学家

contentParagraphs.en: Recently Gingko's scientists have been experimenting with taking things a step further – empowering the robot to be the scientist.
contentParagraphs.zh: 最近，Gingko 的科学家们一直在尝试更进一步——让机器人成为科学家。

contentParagraphs.en: "The really, really wild moment was the first time I saw a lab notebook entry written by the model," says Reshma Shetty, another of the founders.
contentParagraphs.zh: 另一位创始人雷什玛·谢蒂 (Reshma Shetty) 表示：“真正非常疯狂的时刻是我第一次看到该模特在实验室笔记本上写下的条目。”

contentParagraphs.en: Shetty recently worked on a collaboration with OpenAI. Working through ChatGPT, they challenged the bot to create a certain protein. Typically, this level of thinking is left to the scientists, not unlike writing a recipe and handing it to a robot to execute. Now they were asking the bot to write the recipe for them.
contentParagraphs.zh: Shetty 最近与 OpenAI 进行了合作。他们通过 ChatGPT 向机器人发起挑战，要求其创造某种蛋白质。通常，这种思考水平是留给科学家的，就像写一个菜谱并将其交给机器人来执行一样。现在他们要求机器人为他们写菜谱。

contentParagraphs.en: "We had no idea if it would even be able to make protein," says Shetty.
contentParagraphs.zh: “我们甚至不知道它是否能够制造蛋白质，”谢蒂说。

contentParagraphs.en: The bot performed better than they expected. In comparison to human work, they concluded the protein synthesis was a 40 percent reduction in costs. It ran more than 30,000 experiments in 6 months. They've published these results, though the paper has not been peer reviewed.
contentParagraphs.zh: 该机器人的表现比他们预期的要好。与人类工作相比，他们得出的结论是，蛋白质合成的成本降低了 40%。它在 6 个月内进行了 30,000 多次实验。他们已经发表了这些结果，尽管该论文尚未经过同行评审。

contentParagraphs.en: Both Shetty and Kelly stress that humans are still needed to provide the right questions and constraints for experiments. Still, Shetty says it has already fundamentally changed the way she practices science.
contentParagraphs.zh: 谢蒂和凯利都强调，仍然需要人类为实验提供正确的问题和约束。尽管如此，谢蒂表示，这已经从根本上改变了她实践科学的方式。

contentParagraphs.en: Most of the robots in this lab do not resemble humans, and they perform their work while encased in glass.
contentParagraphs.zh: 该实验室中的大多数机器人与人类不同，它们在玻璃中执行工作。

contentParagraphs.en: Jodi Hilton for NPR
contentParagraphs.zh: 乔迪·希尔顿 NPR

contentParagraphs.en: Jodi Hilton for NPR
contentParagraphs.zh: 乔迪·希尔顿 NPR

contentParagraphs.en: "Normally, I rush through designing my experiment because I need to get it done so that I can actually do all the pipetting in the lab and set it all up," says Shetty.
contentParagraphs.zh: “通常情况下，我会匆忙地设计我的实验，因为我需要完成它，这样我才能真正在实验室中进行所有移液操作并将其全部设置好，”谢蒂说。

contentParagraphs.en: Now, she says, she spends more time designing her experiments so that the robot can do them for her overnight.
contentParagraphs.zh: 她说，现在她花更多的时间设计实验，以便机器人可以在一夜之间为她完成这些实验。

contentParagraphs.en: New access to science comes with risks
contentParagraphs.zh: 新的科学途径伴随着风险

contentParagraphs.en: Some people warn these new freedoms bring new dangers. Drew Endy, who studies bioengineering at Stanford, says that artificial intelligence opens the door to the possibility of people with little to no training in science running experiments with questionable goals.
contentParagraphs.zh: 一些人警告说，这些新的自由会带来新的危险。在斯坦福大学研究生物工程的德鲁·恩迪 (Drew Endy) 表示，人工智能为那些几乎没有受过科学训练的人打开了大门，让他们有可能进行带有可疑目标的实验。

contentParagraphs.en: He and some colleagues recently wrote a report illustrating the ways artificial intelligence could be used to do things like mass-p
contentParagraphs.zh: 他和一些同事最近写了一份报告，阐述了人工智能可以用来做大规模生产等事情的方法。

articles.title: Zelenskyy asks Putin for meeting: What’s he offering, could Russia accept?
articles.summary: Russia and Ukraine have been holding peace talks since the war began in 2022, but with no concrete outcome.
articles.titleZh: 泽连斯基邀请普京会面：他提出了什么，俄罗斯可以接受吗？
articles.summaryZh: 自2022年战争爆发以来，俄罗斯和乌克兰一直在举行和平谈判，但没有取得具体成果。

contentParagraphs.en: Ukraine’s President Volodymyr Zelenskyy has written an open letter to Russia’s President Vladimir Putin and proposed a meeting to discuss ending Moscow’s four-year war on Kyiv.The letter, which was published in full on the Ukrainian president’s website on Thursday and sent via diplomatic channels to Russia as well as countries including the United States, comes as Russia’s war on Ukraine continues to rage into its fifth year.Recommended Stories list of 3 itemslist 1 of 3Could Russia hit northern Europe if it gained control of Arctic’s Bear Gap?list 2 of 3Russia launches massive overnight attack on Ukrainelist 3 of 3Ukraine drone attack kills 8 in Russian-held territory in new escalationend of listOn Thursday, at least 12 people were killed and dozens injured in Russian attacks across Ukraine, according to Ukrainian authorities. Zelenskyy also commemorated at least 707 children killed by Russian attacks during the two countries’ more than four-year-long war.Meanwhile, in Russian-occupied Ukraine, at least four people were killed in Ukrainian drone attacks on Thursday. Ukraine also struck an oil complex and naval base in St Petersburg, Russia, on Wednesday.Russia and Ukraine have been holding indirect peace talks since the war began in February 2022, but with little or no concrete outcomes. US President Donald Trump has also met both Putin and Zelenskyy, seeking to bring them to the negotiating table to discuss ending the war, but so far, his efforts have not borne fruit.Will Zelenskyy’s open letter to Putin open a new path to a ceasefire?Here’s what we know:What did Zelenskyy say in his letter?In his letter, Zelenskyy told Putin that he has spent nearly half of his 26 years in power in Russia “waging war against Ukraine” and said Russians are now growing increasingly tired of Ukrainian missiles and drone attacks, inflation and fuel shortages.“We can all see that Russians are finally becoming less comfortable with this reality – with the fact that the war is bringing more and more negative consequences to Russia,” he wrote.He also told Putin that a prolonged war could threaten the Russian president’s personal position. “It is a fact of Russian history that you know well: When Russia grows tired, change comes,” he said.“After 26 years in power, age is beginning to take its toll. And with time, the fatigue with you will only grow,” he added.Zelenskyy also wrote that while he and the Ukrainian people are less concerned about the loss of Russian lives on the battlefield, every loss of a Ukrainian counts. “Even when the ratio of Ukrainian losses to Russian losses is one to five or one to six, it still matters greatly.“We in Ukraine do not want a permanent war. We know very well that life without war is infinitely better. And we want to achieve that,” he said.Zelenskyy noted that with the US focused on its war on Iran, “it would be wrong to simply wait until the war in Europe returns to the centre of its attention,” and suggested a path to peace.“Do not be afraid to take the path out of this war. That is the main thing that is required of you now.“Ukraine proposes ending this war through direct engagement between us – and you. I am proposing a meeting… If you do not personally come to the conclusion that it is time to end this war, Ukraine will continue fighting for its existence,” he added.Regarding the location of the meeting, Zelenskyy said: “There are countries that have traditionally hosted leaders to resolve issues of war and peace. Switzerland, Turkiye, the countries of the Arab world – many are able and willing to host such a meeting.“We believe Europe should be part of this process – those who truly have the capacity to influence the situation. We also believe that the United States must be part of the process. This is what could help shape a new security architecture for our part of the world,” he added.Writing on X, Ukrainian Foreign Minister Andrii Sybiha described the letter as “a serious and meaningful proposal to end the war … with clear, doable steps and an invitation for a personal meeting”.“We expect a meaningful response to this proposal. It’s time to end this war. It’s time to choose peace.”Why is this letter significant?Markus Ziener, a senior visiting fellow at the German Marshall Fund of the United States Berlin office, told Al Jazeera that making the letter public forces Russia to openly show its hand while proving to the world that Ukraine is ready for diplomacy.“It places the moral high ground and the pressure to respond entirely on the Kremlin,” he said.He noted, however, that this is not the first time Zelenskyy has reached out to Putin.“Most recently on May 11, 2025, he publicly and explicitly announced that he was ready to travel to Turkey to meet Putin in person for face-to-face talks,” he said.Zelenskyy’s new proposal shows that the Ukrainian president “feels emboldened by the successful military pushbacks against the Russian army on the battlefield, including successfully targeting Russian infrastructure far in Russian mainland”, Ziener said.“The Ukrainian president believes that, currently, the momentum is on his side and therefore it is good moment [to] offering talks.”Has Putin accepted Zelenskyy’s proposal?The Russian president’s spokesman told reporters in Russia that Putin was aware of Zelenskyy’s letter but had not yet been briefed about its content in detail.But at Russia’s showcase annual economic forum in St Petersburg on Thursday, Putin said his troops were continuing to advance in Ukraine.“The offensive is ongoing on a daily basis. At present, the Russian Federation has taken full control of the Luhansk People’s Republic – 100 percent. And Russia has brought more than 85 percent of the territory of the Donetsk People’s Republic under its control. (And) 80 percent of the territory of the Zaporizhzhia region,” he said, referring to three of the four regions in Ukraine which Moscow has claimed as its own since 2022.“Naturally, under these circumstances,
contentParagraphs.zh: 乌克兰总统弗拉基米尔·泽伦斯基向俄罗斯总统弗拉基米尔·普京写了一封公开信，提议召开一次会议，讨论结束莫斯科对基辅长达四年的战争。这封信于周四在乌克兰总统的网站上全文发布，并通过外交渠道发送给俄罗斯以及包括美国在内的国家，此时俄罗斯对乌克兰的战争已进入第五个年头。推荐故事列表 3 项列表 1 of 3 俄罗斯能否打击如果控制了北极的熊峡，北欧会发生什么？list 2 of 3 俄罗斯对乌克兰发动大规模夜间袭击list 3 of 3 乌克兰无人机袭击在俄罗斯控制的领土上造成8人死亡，新一轮升级结束据乌克兰当局称，周四，俄罗斯在乌克兰各地发动袭击，造成至少12人死亡，数十人受伤。泽连斯基还纪念了在两国长达四年多的战争中，至少 707 名因俄罗斯袭击而丧生的儿童。 与此同时，在俄罗斯占领的乌克兰，周四至少有 4 人在乌克兰无人机袭击中丧生。乌克兰周三还袭击了俄罗斯圣彼得堡的一座石油设施和海军基地。自 2022 年 2 月战争爆发以来，俄罗斯和乌克兰一直在举行间接和谈，但几乎没有取得任何具体成果。美国总统特朗普也同时会见了普京和泽连斯基，试图将他们拉到谈判桌上讨论结束战争，但迄今为止，他的努力尚未取得成果。泽连斯基给普京的公开信是否会开辟一条新的停火之路？我们所知道的是：泽连斯基在信中说了什么？泽连斯基在信中告诉普京，他执政26年的近一半时间都在“发动战争”他写道：“俄罗斯人现在越来越厌倦乌克兰的导弹和无人机袭击、通货膨胀和燃料短缺。我们都可以看到，俄罗斯人最终对这一现实越来越不舒服——事实上，战争给俄罗斯带来了越来越多的负面后果。”他还告诉普京，长期战争可能会威胁到俄罗斯总统的个人地位。他说：“俄罗斯历史上有一个众所周知的事实：当俄罗斯感到疲倦时，变革就会到来。执政26年之后，年龄开始产生影响。随着时间的推移，你的疲劳只会越来越严重。”泽连斯基还写道，虽然他和乌克兰人民不太关心俄罗斯在战场上的生命损失，但每一个乌克兰人的牺牲都很重要。 “即使乌克兰的损失与俄罗斯的损失之比是一比五或一比六，这仍然很重要。”我们乌克兰不希望发生永久战争。我们深知，没有战争的生活会更加美好。我们希望实现这一目标，”他说。泽伦斯基指出，由于美国专注于对伊朗的战争，“仅仅等到欧洲战争重新成为其关注中心是错误的”，并提出了一条通往和平的道路。“不要害怕走出这场战争。”这是现在对你们的主要要求。他补充道：“乌克兰提议通过我们和你们之间的直接接触来结束这场战争。我提议召开一次会议……如果你们个人没有得出结束这场战争的结论，乌克兰将继续为自己的生存而战。”关于会议地点，泽伦斯基说：“有些国家历来接待领导人来解决战争与和平问题。瑞士、土耳其、阿拉伯世界国家——许多国家都能够并愿意主办这样的会议。“我们相信欧洲应该成为这一进程的一部分——那些真正有能力影响局势的人。我们还认为，美国必须参与这一进程。这有助于为我们地区塑造新的安全架构。”他补充道。乌克兰外交部长安德里·西比哈在 X 上撰文称，这封信是“一份严肃而有意义的结束战争提议……包含明确、可行的步骤，并邀请举行个人会晤”。“我们期待这一提议得到有意义的回应。是时候结束这场战争了。是时候选择和平了。“为什么这封信意义重大？德国马歇尔基金会柏林办事处高级访问研究员马库斯·齐纳告诉半岛电视台，公开这封信迫使俄罗斯公开亮出自己的手，同时向世界证明乌克兰已做好外交准备。”他说，“它把道义上的制高点和完全回应克里姆林宫的压力放在了克里姆林宫身上。”不过，他指出，这并不是泽连斯基第一次向俄罗斯伸出援手。普京。他说：“最近，2025 年 5 月 11 日，他公开明确宣布，他准备前往土耳其亲自会见普京，进行面对面会谈。”齐纳表示，泽连斯基的新提议表明，乌克兰总统“因在战场上对俄罗斯军队的成功军事反击而感到鼓舞，包括成功地针对俄罗斯本土遥远的俄罗斯基础设施”。普京站在他一边，因此现在是提出会谈的好时机。”普京接受了泽连斯基的提议吗？俄罗斯总统发言人告诉俄罗斯记者，普京知道泽连斯基的信，但尚未详细了解其内容。但周四在圣彼得堡举行的俄罗斯年度经济论坛上，普京表示，他的军队正在继续向乌克兰推进。“攻势每天都在进行。目前，俄罗斯联邦已完全控制卢甘斯克人民共和国——100%。俄罗斯已经控制了顿涅茨克人民共和国85%以上的领土。 （以及）扎波罗热地区 80% 的领土，”他说，指的是莫斯科自 2022 年以来声称拥有主权的乌克兰四个地区中的三个。“自然，在这种情况下，

articles.title: Most K-12 teachers say AI's impact on education will eclipse the internet or computers
articles.summary: A new NPR/Ipsos poll shows many teachers are using AI to save time, but a majority are also worried the technology is making it harder for students to learn to think for themselves.
articles.titleZh: 大多数 K-12 教师表示，人工智能对教育的影响将超越互联网或计算机
articles.summaryZh: NPR/Ipsos 的一项新民意调查显示，许多教师正在使用人工智能来节省时间，但大多数人也担心该技术会让学生更难学会独立思考。

contentParagraphs.en: Gracia Lam for NPR
contentParagraphs.zh: NPR 的格雷西亚·林 (Gracia Lam)

contentParagraphs.en: The effects of artificial intelligence on learning are still largely unclear. But a new NPR/Ipsos poll of K-12 teachers found that nearly 3-in-4 believe AI has bigger implications for education than past innovations like the internet or computers.
contentParagraphs.zh: 人工智能对学习的影响在很大程度上仍不清楚。但 NPR/Ipsos 对 K-12 教师进行的一项新民意调查发现，近四分之三的人认为人工智能对教育的影响比互联网或计算机等过去的创新更大。

contentParagraphs.en: The nationally representative poll surveyed 545 respondents and paints a complex picture of teachers' views on AI: Many are using it to save time and improve their teaching materials, but a majority of teachers are worried AI is making it harder for students to learn to think for themselves.
contentParagraphs.zh: 这项具有全国代表性的民意调查对 545 名受访者进行了调查，描绘了教师对人工智能的复杂看法：许多人利用人工智能来节省时间和改进教材，但大多数教师担心人工智能让学生更难学会独立思考。

contentParagraphs.en: "We're in an environment where teachers feel like this is going to fundamentally reshape the future of education moving forward," says Mallory Newall, a senior vice president at Ipsos. "They have serious concerns about AI's impact on how they relate to their students and how students relate to each other."
contentParagraphs.zh: 益普索 (Ipsos) 高级副总裁马洛里·纽厄尔 (Mallory Newall) 表示：“在我们所处的环境中，教师们认为这将从根本上重塑教育的未来。” “他们非常担心人工智能会对他们与学生的关系以及学生之间的关系产生影响。”

contentParagraphs.en: And schools have a role to play: A resounding majority of polled teachers — nearly 8-in-10 — think schools should teach responsible use of AI.
contentParagraphs.zh: 学校也可以发挥作用：绝大多数接受调查的教师（近十分之八）认为学校应该教授如何负责任地使用人工智能。

contentParagraphs.en: "To me, that sends a very clear message that teachers are acknowledging that AI is having humongous implications on education as we know it," says Newall. "It's not going away. And so now is the time to act."
contentParagraphs.zh: “对我来说，这发出了一个非常明确的信息，即教师们承认人工智能正在对我们所知的教育产生巨大的影响，”纽厄尔说。 “它不会消失。所以现在是采取行动的时候了。”

contentParagraphs.en: More of a teacher's helper than a classroom tool
contentParagraphs.zh: 与其说是课堂工具，不如说是老师的帮手

contentParagraphs.en: The poll shows students aren't widely using AI in the classroom – at least not yet. A little more than half of teachers say the technology isn't being used in class by students at all, while about 2-in-5 teachers say students are using it in class at least once a week.
contentParagraphs.zh: 民意调查显示，学生们并没有在课堂上广泛使用人工智能——至少现在还没有。略多于一半的教师表示，学生根本没有在课堂上使用该技术，而大约五分之二的教师表示，学生每周至少在课堂上使用一次该技术。

contentParagraphs.en: Meanwhile, a majority of teachers polled — 6-in-10 — say they've used AI themselves to help with work tasks.
contentParagraphs.zh: 与此同时，大多数接受调查的教师（十分之六）表示，他们自己已经使用人工智能来帮助完成工作任务。

contentParagraphs.en: Michele Naber, a veteran biology teacher at El Toro High School in Orange County, Calif., says she allows her students to use AI during certain lessons to teach them how to properly prompt chatbots and verify their accuracy.
contentParagraphs.zh: 加利福尼亚州奥兰治县埃尔托罗高中的资深生物老师米歇尔·纳伯 (Michele Naber) 表示，她允许学生在某些课程中使用人工智能，教他们如何正确提示聊天机器人并验证其准确性。

contentParagraphs.en: For example, she says she'll tell her students to ask ChatGPT to describe a particular animal's physical characteristics and habitat, then verify what the chatbot generated with reliable sources. She says the lesson shows students that AI still gets it wrong sometimes.
contentParagraphs.zh: 例如，她说她会告诉学生要求 ChatGPT 描述特定动物的身体特征和栖息地，然后通过可靠来源验证聊天机器人生成的内容。她说，这堂课向学生展示了人工智能有时仍然会出错。

contentParagraphs.en: "That's one of the things that has to be taught: You can't take it literally," Naber says.
contentParagraphs.zh: “这是必须教的事情之一：你不能从字面上理解它，”纳伯说。

contentParagraphs.en: She says she's also had success using AI to generate multiple choice questions for assessments.
contentParagraphs.zh: 她说她还成功地使用人工智能生成多项选择题进行评估。

contentParagraphs.en: "That's something that normally, as a teacher, would have taken you probably upwards of an hour … and it minimized the entire task to five minutes. That's helpful."
contentParagraphs.zh: “作为一名老师，这通常会花费你一个小时以上的时间……而它将整个任务减少到五分钟。这很有帮助。”

contentParagraphs.en: A majority of surveyed teachers who report using AI on work-related tasks say it saves them time, but most — 63% — say that time savings equates to two hours or less per week.
contentParagraphs.zh: 大多数接受调查的教师在工作相关任务中使用人工智能表示，这节省了他们的时间，但大多数（63%）表示，节省的时间相当于每周两个小时或更少。

contentParagraphs.en: Joann Purcell, a math teacher and instructional coach at Downers Grove North High School, in the Chicago suburbs, says she's found AI useful for coming up with professional development activities for her fellow educators.
contentParagraphs.zh: 芝加哥郊区唐纳斯格罗夫北高中 (Downers Grove North High School) 的数学老师兼教学教练乔安·珀塞尔 (Joann Purcell) 表示，她发现人工智能有助于为她的教育同行们开展专业发展活动。

contentParagraphs.en: But she doesn't use AI with her students. And Purcell says it isn't reliable enough to generate math questions.
contentParagraphs.zh: 但她并没有对她的学生使用人工智能。珀塞尔表示，它不够可靠，无法生成数学问题。

contentParagraphs.en: "It's a pain in the butt to go through and see where the mistakes are, and I feel like if I have to do that, I might as well just write the question myself," Purcell says.
contentParagraphs.zh: “检查并找出错误在哪里是一件很痛苦的事情，我觉得如果我必须这样做，我还不如自己写这个问题，”珀塞尔说。

contentParagraphs.en: Are students learning to think for themselves?
contentParagraphs.zh: 学生是否学会独立思考？

contentParagraphs.en: More than half, 54%, of polled teachers say AI makes it harder for students to learn critical thinking skills.
contentParagraphs.zh: 超过一半（54%）的受访教师表示，人工智能让学生更难学习批判性思维技能。

contentParagraphs.en: Christa Corricelli, a special education teacher at Saugus Middle/High School outside Boston, says AI could be a valuable technology for learning, but too often students are using it as an answer machine — not a tool to bolster their thinking.
contentParagraphs.zh: 波士顿郊外索格斯中学/高中的特殊教育老师 Christa Corricelli 表示，人工智能可能是一种有价值的学习技术，但学生常常将其用作答题机，而不是支持他们思考的工具。

contentParagraphs.en: "I think students who aren't already intrinsically self-motivated to be critical thinkers, like that top 1% of the class … I think people who are not already that personality type, we're going to see those critical thinking skills atrophy over time," Corricelli says.
contentParagraphs.zh: “我认为那些还没有从本质上自我激励成为批判性思考者的学生，比如班上排名前 1% 的学生……我认为那些还没有成为批判性思考者的人，随着时间的推移，我们会看到这些批判性思维技能逐渐萎缩，”Corricelli 说。

contentParagraphs.en: Naber, in California, feels a deep responsibility to teach her students that humans must always interrogate and verify what AI generates.
contentParagraphs.zh: 加利福尼亚州的纳伯深感有责任教导她的学生，人类必须始终询问和验证人工智能生成的内容。

contentParagraphs.en: "I care about [my students]. I want them to be able to look at the world and figure out things for themselves, not rely on a piece of software," she says. "If we stop questioning what it says, we can be led to believe anything. And that's what really scares me."
contentParagraphs.zh: “我关心[我的学生]。我希望他们能够观察世界并自己解决问题，而不是依赖某个软件，”她说。 “如果我们停止质疑它所说的内容，我们就会被引导去相信任何事情。这才是真正让我害怕的地方。”

contentParagraphs.en: More than half of teachers polled — 55% — think AI is mostly just a shortcut for students to avoid doing more work.
contentParagraphs.zh: 超过一半的受访教师（55%）认为人工智能主要只是学生避免做更多作业的捷径。

contentParagraphs.en: However, Ellie Rodriguez, a special education teacher at Royal Palm Beach Community High School near Palm Beach, Fla., says AI can be especially helpful for students with disabilities. She explains that one of her students, who is on the autism spectrum, recently used AI to get help with an assignment.
contentParagraphs.zh: 然而，佛罗里达州棕榈滩附近皇家棕榈滩社区高中的特殊教育老师埃莉·罗德里格斯表示，人工智能对残疾学生特别有帮助。她解释说，她的一名患有自闭症谱系的学生最近使用人工智能来获得作业帮助。

contentParagraphs.en: "I praised him," Rodriguez says, because he wouldn't have been able to complete the assignment without the help AI provided.
contentParagraphs.zh: “我赞扬了他，”罗德里格斯说，因为如果没有人工智能提供的帮助，他就无法完成任务。

contentParagraphs.en: "It got him to do the work, but hopefully, too, it helped him to apply using resources – like you would use an encyclopedia, like you would use a library book – to find your answers," she says.
contentParagraphs.zh: “这让他完成了这项工作，但希望这也能帮助他利用资源——就像你会使用百科全书，就像你会使用图书馆的书籍一样——来寻找答案，”她说。

contentParagraphs.en: But Rodriguez does worry the technology could hamper learning for students who don't have disabilities or who are capable of doing assignments without the assistance of AI.
contentParagraphs.zh: 但罗德里格斯确实担心这项技术可能会妨碍那些没有残疾或能够在没有人工智能帮助的情况下完成作业的学生的学习。

contentParagraphs.en: And she says she and her colleagues, including some English teachers, are deeply concerned about the impact AI is having on students' ability to think for themselves.
contentParagraphs.zh: 她说，她和她的同事，包括一些英语老师，都非常担心人工智能对学生独立思考能力的影响。

contentParagraphs.en: AI is eroding student-teacher trust
contentParagraphs.zh: 人工智能正在侵蚀师生信任

contentParagraphs.en: Nearly 6-in-10 surveyed educators say AI is eroding the level of trust between students and teachers. About 4-in-10 say they've required more assignments to be done by hand, and 4-in-10 also say they've required more assignments to be done in class as a result of AI.
contentParagraphs.zh: 近十分之六的接受调查的教育工作者表示，人工智能正在侵蚀学生和教师之间的信任程度。大约十分之四的人表示他们需要更多手工完成的作业，十分之四的人还表示他们需要通过人工智能在课堂上完成更多作业。

contentParagraphs.en: Newall, with Ipsos, says the erosion of trust caused by AI is "one of the biggest red flags in the data."
contentParagraphs.zh: 益普索的纽厄尔表示，人工智能造成的信任侵蚀是“数据中最大的危险信号之一”。

contentParagraphs.en: She says that issue is compounded by another survey finding: 70% of teachers believe the public's perception of them has gotten worse.
contentParagraphs.zh: 她说，另一项调查结果使这个问题变得更加复杂：70% 的教师认为公众对他们的看法变得更糟。

contentParagraphs.en: "What that tells me is that they are trying to navigate some very compl
contentParagraphs.zh: “这告诉我，他们正在努力应对一些非常复杂的问题。

articles.title: For many fans, the World Cup begins with a pack of stickers. The U.S. is catching up
articles.summary: Collecting Panini stickers is a World Cup tradition beloved in Europe and Latin America. In the U.S., interest has been building steadily, and this summer, the buzz is bigger than ever.
articles.titleZh: 对于许多球迷来说，世界杯是从一包贴纸开始的。美国正在迎头赶上
articles.summaryZh: 收集帕尼尼贴纸是欧洲和拉丁美洲喜爱的世界杯传统。在美国，人们的兴趣一直在稳步增长，今年夏天，人们的关注度比以往任何时候都高。

contentParagraphs.en: A sticker enthusiast shows off some of the FIFA World Cup 2026 Panini stickers bought at the Soccer Locker on June 2 in Miami.
contentParagraphs.zh: 一名贴纸爱好者展示了 6 月 2 日在迈阿密 Soccer Locker 购买的一些 FIFA 2026 世界杯帕尼尼贴纸。

contentParagraphs.en: NEW YORK — In Brian Sanchez's slice of Astoria, the FIFA World Cup doesn't begin with the first match. It starts weeks earlier, with the arrival of a sticker album — and a mission.
contentParagraphs.zh: 纽约——在布莱恩·桑切斯 (Brian Sanchez) 的阿斯托里亚地区，国际足联世界杯并不从第一场比赛开始。一切从几周前开始，伴随着一张贴纸专辑的到来——以及一个任务。

contentParagraphs.en: It's a deceptively simple one: Fill the book with all the stickers representing World Cup teams, players, venues and other tournament details. But these stickers are sold in blind packs, similar to baseball or Pokémon cards, which adds to the fun and the headaches.
contentParagraphs.zh: 这是一个看似简单的方法：在书里填满代表世界杯球队、球员、场地和其他比赛细节的所有贴纸。但这些贴纸是盲装出售的，类似于棒球卡或神奇宝贝卡，这既增加了乐趣，也增加了麻烦。

contentParagraphs.en: Sanchez, 20, has tried to complete the task before but never succeeded. This year, he planned to skip it altogether, but it was hard to ignore the chatter and excitement among his friends and family — both at home and abroad — who were all participating.
contentParagraphs.zh: 20岁的桑切斯此前曾尝试完成这项任务，但从未成功。今年，他计划完全跳过它，但很难忽视他的国内外朋友和家人的喋喋不休和兴奋，他们都参加了。

contentParagraphs.en: "Honestly it comes down to a little bit of FOMO," he said.
contentParagraphs.zh: “老实说，这归结为一点 FOMO，”他说。

contentParagraphs.en: The hunt for stickers, produced by the Italian company Panini, is a decades-old World Cup tradition that's especially popular in Latin America and Europe. In the U.S., interest has been building steadily over the years, but this summer, the buzz is bigger than ever.
contentParagraphs.zh: 寻找贴纸由意大利帕尼尼公司生产，是已有数十年历史的世界杯传统，在拉丁美洲和欧洲尤其受欢迎。在美国，人们的兴趣多年来一直在稳步增长，但今年夏天，人们的关注度比以往任何时候都高。

contentParagraphs.en: Jason Howarth, senior vice president of marketing and athlete relations at Panini America, said retailers reported being sold out of sticker packets within a week of the release in late April — unseen in previous World Cup cycles.
contentParagraphs.zh: 帕尼尼美国公司营销和运动员关系高级副总裁贾森·豪沃斯 (Jason Howarth) 表示，零售商报告称，四月下旬发布后一周内，贴纸包就被卖光了——这在之前的世界杯周期中是没有见过的。

contentParagraphs.en: "There's a different energy coming out of it," he said. "Right now, it's outpacing where we were in 2022 by three to five times."
contentParagraphs.zh: “它散发出一种不同的能量，”他说。 “目前，它的速度是 2022 年的三到五倍。”

contentParagraphs.en: The surging demand comes as collectors face their toughest challenge yet. This year, they need to track down 980 distinct stickers to put the album to bed — 310 more than at the 2022 World Cup and a record number for the company. It's a reflection of the upcoming tournament's historic scale, which is expanding from 32 teams to 48 across three countries.
contentParagraphs.zh: 需求激增之际，收藏家面临着迄今为止最严峻的挑战。今年，他们需要找到 980 个不同的贴纸才能完成专辑，比 2022 年世界杯多了 310 个，这也是该公司的创纪录数字。这反映了即将举行的锦标赛的历史性规模，参赛队伍将从 32 支球队扩大到 3 个国家的 48 支球队。

contentParagraphs.en: Softcover sticker albums are displayed during at FIFA World Cup event at a Boys & Girls Club in Dallas on April 24.
contentParagraphs.zh: 4 月 24 日，达拉斯男孩女孩俱乐部举行的 FIFA 世界杯活动期间展示了平装贴纸专辑。

contentParagraphs.en: This edition will also be the second to last men's World Cup sticker album produced by Panini — ending a partnership that stretches back over five decades. Last month, FIFA announced that starting in 2031, U.S.-based Fanatics will be the official supplier of FIFA soccer cards, trading cards and stickers.
contentParagraphs.zh: 该版本也将是帕尼尼制作的倒数第二本男子世界杯贴纸专辑，结束了长达五年的合作关系。上个月，国际足联宣布从 2031 年开始，美国 Fanatics 将成为国际足联足球卡、交易卡和贴纸的官方供应商。

contentParagraphs.en: On a recent afternoon in Central Park, Sanchez met up with other collectors. Hunched over stacks of stickers, some two dozen people inspected the offerings with laser focus.
contentParagraphs.zh: 最近的一个下午，桑切斯在中央公园会见了其他收藏家。大约有两打人弯腰看着成堆的贴纸，用激光聚焦检查这些产品。

contentParagraphs.en: With only four stickers missing, Sanchez was already looking forward to earning bragging rights as the first person in his family across the finish line this year.
contentParagraphs.zh: 由于只缺少四张贴纸，桑切斯已经期待着成为今年家里第一个冲过终点线的人。

contentParagraphs.en: " I'm feeling pretty accomplished," he said. "I've been trying to get a win, and this is gonna be a huge win for me."
contentParagraphs.zh: “我感觉很有成就感，”他说。 “我一直在努力赢得胜利，这对我来说将是一次巨大的胜利。”

contentParagraphs.en: An expensive, labor-intensive but rewarding hobby
contentParagraphs.zh: 一项昂贵、劳动密集型但有益的爱好

contentParagraphs.en: A single pack of seven stickers — available online, at corner stores or drugstore chains like Walgreens and CVS — now cost $2, compared to four years ago when five stickers retailed for around $1. That means simply buying enough packs to accumulate 980 stickers would total $280.
contentParagraphs.zh: 一包 7 张贴纸（可在网上、街角商店或 Walgreens 和 CVS 等连锁药店购买）现在售价 2 美元，而四年前，5 张贴纸的零售价约为 1 美元。这意味着只需购买足够多的包即可累积 980 张贴纸，总计 280 美元。

contentParagraphs.en: Given the costs, finishing the book is rarely a solitary pursuit, and aficionados often meet up to spread the wealth, according to Crista Latvis, 26, who organized the recent sticker swap in Central Park.
contentParagraphs.zh: 26 岁的克里斯塔·拉特维斯最近在中央公园组织了贴纸交换活动，她表示，考虑到成本，完成这本书很少是一个人的追求，书迷们经常会聚在一起分享财富。

contentParagraphs.en: "You can't just buy your way into it," she said. "Otherwise, it's super expensive and you've got to be very lucky."
contentParagraphs.zh: “你不能只靠花钱买路，”她说。 “否则，它会非常昂贵，而且你必须非常幸运。”

contentParagraphs.en: For many, these gatherings are part of the pastime's draw.
contentParagraphs.zh: 对于许多人来说，这些聚会是消遣的一部分。

contentParagraphs.en: "It's great to meet other people who are also doing it and also excited for the World Cup, especially since it's here," Latvis said.
contentParagraphs.zh: 拉特维斯说：“很高兴见到其他也在做这件事并对世界杯感到兴奋的人，尤其是因为它在这里。”

contentParagraphs.en: Sebastian Clavijo, who attended Latvis' swap, said he spent tens of thousands of dollars on his quest this year. Clavijo, 32, has been collecting Panini stickers since he was 4. This year, his goal is to complete the book only with pieces featuring red and purple borders — an even rarer get.
contentParagraphs.zh: 参加拉脱维亚交换的塞巴斯蒂安·克拉维霍(Sebastian Clavijo)表示，今年他在这项任务上花费了数万美元。 32 岁的克拉维霍从 4 岁起就开始收集帕尼尼贴纸。今年，他的目标是只用带有红色和紫色边框的贴纸来完成这本书——这是一种更加罕见的作品。

contentParagraphs.en: " I just like soccer and I love collecting," he said. "That's my hobby, you know?"
contentParagraphs.zh: “我喜欢足球，也喜欢收藏，”他说。 “那是我的爱好，你知道吗？”

contentParagraphs.en: In 2022, Panini introduced stickers with different colored borders that vary in rarity. That element has been an especially big hit with the trading card community and contributed to the hobby's appeal in the U.S., according to Howarth from Panini America.
contentParagraphs.zh: 2022 年，帕尼尼推出了带有不同颜色边框、稀有程度不同的贴纸。帕尼尼美国公司的霍沃斯表示，这一元素在集换式卡牌界受到了特别大的欢迎，并提高了这项爱好在美国的吸引力。

contentParagraphs.en: Panini popularity has grown along with soccer
contentParagraphs.zh: 帕尼尼的受欢迎程度随着足球的发展而增长

contentParagraphs.en: Demand has always existed in New York, Texas, Florida, among other big states, but it's also emerging nationwide, in places like Phoenix and the Northwest, according to Howarth.
contentParagraphs.zh: 豪沃斯表示，纽约州、德克萨斯州、佛罗里达州等大州的需求一直存在，但在全国范围内，例如菲尼克斯和西北地区，这种需求也在出现。

contentParagraphs.en: " As soccer has grown, so has Panini," he said.
contentParagraphs.zh: “随着足球的发展，帕尼尼也在发展，”他说。

contentParagraphs.en: Howarth believes part of this year's popularity stems from the expanded World Cup format. Teams that have never qualified for the tournament — and therefore never been sticker-fied by Panini — are finally getting their moment.
contentParagraphs.zh: 豪沃斯认为，今年的受欢迎程度部分源于世界杯赛制的扩大。那些从未获得过锦标赛资格、因此也从未被帕尼尼贴上贴纸的球队终于迎来了属于自己的时刻。

contentParagraphs.en: A boy opens packets of FIFA World Cup 2026 Panini stickers that his father bought him at the Soccer Locker on June 2 in Miami.
contentParagraphs.zh: 6 月 2 日，一名男孩打开父亲在迈阿密足球储物柜给他买的 2026 年 FIFA 世界杯帕尼尼贴纸。

contentParagraphs.en: For some, completing the sticker album is driven by nostalgia for their childhood, family or home country.
contentParagraphs.zh: 对于一些人来说，完成贴纸相册是出于对童年、家庭或祖国的怀念。

contentParagraphs.en: Linda Lino never heard of the hobby until she was 18, and her grandmother gave her a Panini sticker book. That was in 2014. Lino has completed every World Cup edition since, in part in memory of her late grandmother.
contentParagraphs.zh: 琳达·利诺 (Linda Lino) 直到 18 岁才听说过这个爱好，她的祖母给了她一本帕尼尼贴纸书。那是在 2014 年。自那以后，利诺完成了每一届世界杯，部分是为了纪念她已故的祖母。

contentParagraphs.en: "It started with my grandma and then it became like a whole family thing," Lino said. "I love the community that it brings together."
contentParagraphs.zh: “这一切都是从我奶奶开始的，然后就变成了全家人的事情，”利诺说。 “我喜欢它聚集在一起的社区。”

contentParagraphs.en: That's especially true with her father, who never had the chance to collect stickers when he was a kid in Peru, Lino said. Now, the two are making up for lost time.
contentParagraphs.zh: 利诺说，她的父亲尤其如此，他小时候在秘鲁从未有机会收集贴纸。现在，两人正在弥补失去的时间。

contentParagraphs.en: "My dad is so excited," she said. "He's like 'I want to help you. I want to put the stickers together.'"
contentParagraphs.zh: “我爸爸很兴奋，”她说。 “他就像‘我想帮助你。我想把贴纸放在一起。’”

contentParagraphs.en: Clemente Lisi, a sports journalist who has written about the Panini sticker phenomenon, said the
contentParagraphs.zh: 曾撰写过有关帕尼尼贴纸现象的体育记者克莱门特·利斯 (Clemente Lisi) 表示，

articles.title: Mangrove forests are healing after decades of human destruction
articles.summary: Swampy mangrove forests are staging a surprise comeback - which is good news for coastal communities and the climate.
articles.titleZh: 经过数十年的人类破坏后，红树林正在恢复
articles.summaryZh: 沼泽红树林正在出人意料地卷土重来——这对沿海社区和气候来说是个好消息。

articles.title: Senate Republicans pass immigration funding after overnight vote
articles.summary: After a marathon 18-hour vote, the Senate has funded immigration enforcement. The GOP bill funds ICE and the Border Patrol for three years.
articles.titleZh: 参议院共和党人隔夜投票后通过移民资金
articles.summaryZh: 经过 18 小时的马拉松式投票后，参议院为移民执法提供了资金。共和党法案为 ICE 和边境巡逻队提供三年资助。

contentParagraphs.en: A view of the U.S. Capitol on June 4, 2026.
contentParagraphs.zh: 2026 年 6 月 4 日美国国会大厦景观。

contentParagraphs.en: After a marathon 18-hour vote, Senate Republicans advanced roughly $70 billion in funding for immigration enforcement agencies that had been carved out of an earlier funding deal to reopen the rest of the Department of Homeland Security. The funds would extend through the remainder of President Trump's time in the White House.
contentParagraphs.zh: 经过长达 18 小时的马拉松式投票后，参议院共和党人为移民执法机构提供了约 700 亿美元的资金，这些资金是根据先前重新开放国土安全部其他部门的资金协议而确定的。这些资金将持续到特朗普总统在白宫的剩余任期。

contentParagraphs.en: One Republican, Sen. Lisa Murkowski of Alaska, voted against it.
contentParagraphs.zh: 一位共和党人、阿拉斯加州参议员丽莎·穆尔科斯基(Lisa Murkowski)投了反对票。

contentParagraphs.en: The package now heads for a vote in the House of Representatives, which could happen as early as next week.
contentParagraphs.zh: 该方案目前将在众议院进行投票，最早可能于下周进行。

contentParagraphs.en: While the Senate passage is a victory for Republicans, who have been trying to pass immigration enforcement for months, the overnight vote-a-rama exposed rifts within their ranks.
contentParagraphs.zh: 虽然参议院的通过对共和党人来说是一次胜利，几个月来他们一直在努力通过移民执法，但一夜之间的拉玛投票暴露了他们内部的裂痕。

contentParagraphs.en: At the center of it all is the Trump administration's proposed $1.8 billion fund to distribute taxpayer dollars to people who allege they have been politically targeted by the government, perhaps including Jan. 6 insurrectionists.
contentParagraphs.zh: 这一切的核心是特朗普政府提议的 18 亿美元基金，用于将纳税人的钱分配给那些声称自己成为政府政治目标的人，其中可能包括 1 月 6 日的叛乱分子。

contentParagraphs.en: The fund originated as part of an out-of-court settlement to resolve a $10 billion lawsuit brought by President Trump against his own government over the 2019 leak of his tax records. It has been unpopular among congressional lawmakers, including Republicans — many of whom were present at the Capitol when it was attacked in 2021.
contentParagraphs.zh: 该基金最初是庭外和解的一部分，旨在解决特朗普总统因 2019 年纳税记录泄露而对其政府提起的 100 亿美元诉讼。它在包括共和党人在内的国会议员中不受欢迎——2021 年国会大厦遭到袭击时，其中许多人都在场。

contentParagraphs.en: "We have a lot of members who are concerned, obviously," Senate Majority Leader John Thune, R-S.D., told reporters last month.
contentParagraphs.zh: “显然，我们有很多成员对此感到担忧，”参议院多数党领袖约翰·图恩（R-S.D.）上个月告诉记者。

contentParagraphs.en: "People are concerned about paying their mortgage or rent, affording groceries and paying for gas, not about putting together a $1.8 billion fund for the President and his allies to pay whomever they wish with no legal precedent or accountability," Sen. Bill Cassidy, R-La., wrote on X. Cassidy recently lost his reelection race to a Trump-backed primary challenger.
contentParagraphs.zh: 路易斯安那州共和党参议员比尔·卡西迪 (Bill Cassidy) 在《X》上写道：“人们关心的是支付抵押贷款或租金、购买食品杂货和支付天然气费用，而不是为总统及其盟友设立 18 亿美元的基金，在没有法律先例或责任的情况下向他们想要的任何人付款。”卡西迪最近在连任竞选中输给了特朗普支持的初选挑战者。

contentParagraphs.en: Todd Blanche, the acting attorney general, sought to ease such concerns during testimony in the House on Tuesday, telling lawmakers the administration was dropping plans for the fund. But President Trump introduced new uncertainty on Wednesday, telling reporters in the Oval Office he was unsure.
contentParagraphs.zh: 代理司法部长托德·布兰奇周二在众议院作证时试图缓解此类担忧，他告诉立法者政府将放弃该基金的计划。但特朗普总统周三引入了新的不确定性，他在椭圆形办公室告诉记者他不确定。

contentParagraphs.en: "I'd have to ask the lawyers," he said. "I don't know."
contentParagraphs.zh: “我得问问律师，”他说。 “我不知道。”

contentParagraphs.en: An effort from Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer Thursday night to send the bill back to the Senate Judiciary Committee to kill the fund was open for several hours on the floor, and had support from three Republicans up for re-election this November: Susan Collins of Maine, Dan Sullivan of Alaska and Jon Husted of Ohio.
contentParagraphs.zh: 周四晚上，参议院少数党领袖查克·舒默(Chuck Schumer)试图将该法案发回参议院司法委员会，以终止该基金，这一努力在会议上进行了几个小时，并得到了三名即将于今年11月连任的共和党人的支持：缅因州的苏珊·柯林斯(Susan Collins)、阿拉斯加州的丹·沙利文(Dan Sullivan)和俄亥俄州的乔恩·赫斯特德(Jon Husted)。

contentParagraphs.en: Republican senators also offered amendments to limit the fund, including an effort from Sen. Thom Tillis, R-N.C., to redirect funds towards fraud enforcement.
contentParagraphs.zh: 共和党参议员还提出了限制该基金的修正案，其中包括北卡罗来纳州共和党参议员托姆·蒂利斯(Thom Tillis)的努力，将资金重新用于欺诈执法。

contentParagraphs.en: Eight GOP senators supported an amendment that would prevent payouts from the fund to Jan. 6 insurrectionists.
contentParagraphs.zh: 八名共和党参议员支持一项修正案，该修正案将阻止该基金向 1 月 6 日的叛乱分子付款。

contentParagraphs.en: Repeated delays
contentParagraphs.zh: 屡次延误

contentParagraphs.en: The fight over the weaponization fund was only the latest controversy to sidetrack the immigration enforcement package, which the president originally asked Congress to pass by June 1.
contentParagraphs.zh: 围绕武器化基金的争论只是偏离移民执法计划的最新争议，总统最初要求国会在 6 月 1 日之前通过该计划。

contentParagraphs.en: Republicans were forced to use a special procedure known as reconciliation to skirt the Senate's de facto 60-vote threshold for most legislation and fund Immigration and Customs Enforcement and Border Patrol after Democrats refused to lend their support.
contentParagraphs.zh: 在民主党拒绝提供支持后，共和党被迫使用一种称为和解的特殊程序来规避参议院大多数立法事实上的 60 票门槛，并为移民和海关执法以及边境巡逻队提供资金。

contentParagraphs.en: Democrats were hoping to force negotiations over reforms to immigration enforcement practices, including restrictions on face-coverings and a body camera mandate, after federal agents killed two American citizens in Minnesota earlier this year. The fight led to the longest agency shutdown in U.S. government history. The Department of Homeland Security was shuttered for 76 days.
contentParagraphs.zh: 今年早些时候，联邦特工在明尼苏达州杀害了两名美国公民，民主党人希望就移民执法改革进行谈判，包括限制蒙面和佩戴随身摄像头。这场斗争导致美国政府历史上最长的机构关闭。国土安全部关闭 76 天。

articles.title: We need to stop AI developing without humans, says Anthropic co-founder
articles.summary: Jack Clark tells BBC's Newsnight AI could get to the point where it develops without human input.
articles.titleZh: Anthropic 联合创始人表示，我们需要阻止人工智能在没有人类的情况下发展
articles.summaryZh: 杰克·克拉克 (Jack Clark) 告诉 BBC 新闻之夜，人工智能可以在没有人类输入的情况下实现发展。

contentParagraphs.en: We need to stop AI developing without humans, says Anthropic co-founder4 hours agoFaisal Islam,economics editorandKali Hays,technology reporterAnthropic co-founder Jack Clark has called for the ability to slow progression of artificial intelligence (AI), warning the technology is nearing a point where it could develop without human input."You want the option to be able to take your foot off the gas and put your foot on the brake", Clark told BBC Newsnight. "Right now, it's like the AI industry has a gas pedal, but it doesn't have a brake pedal."He stressed people, through government policy, need to keep control of AI systems, which will only get more powerful and have broader impacts on society."The world needs to do some thinking and we need to eventually develop some new regulations that allow us to be confident in these systems," he said.Already, Anthropic's popular chatbot Claude is operating on code of which 80% the system wrote itself. Getting to 100% is possible within two years, Clark said, and "would have huge implications".Clark did not outline how a "brake pedal" for AI research and development could be created, but drew a parallel between AI and the oil boom and barons of the turn of the last century."Society's response was to come up with a sensible policy and regulatory framework that gave people confidence in oil and the benefits that oil could provide to the world, and meant that you didn't have to worry about the personalities of the people leading the companies", Clark said. "That's clearly where we end up here."Yet, Anthropic this week welcomed an executive order on AI from US President Donald Trump that was relatively hands-off in its directives toward the companies. Major AI companies pursuing advances in the technology, including Anthropic, OpenAI and Google, have also not said they will pause their own research.It is poised to be one of the first public listings by a newer AI firm and one of the most valuable stock listings in history, as Anthropic's valuation is estimated by private investors to be nearly $1tn (£745bn).Clark said Anthropic's motivation for publicly discussing the growing capability of AI technology is not to further burnish its reputation with paying customers.He simply wants to "tell the world what we're seeing inside these companies with this unusual technology".Bloomberg via Getty ImagesJack Clark was one of seven former OpenAI employees to co-found Anthropic in 2021Since its founding by chief executive Dario Amodei, Clark and a handful of other executives, Anthropic has positioned itself as outspoken about potential risks stemming from AI. It even engaged in a public dispute with the US Department of Defense over concerns that its AI tools would be used in mass surveillance of Americans and autonomous warfare."I am worried for my kids if we as a society don't have a serious conversation about what the implications of AI's continued advances mean," Clark told Newsnight. "There are potentially great benefits. There are also risks."Clark added that one of the risks is a disruption to the economy, with fears that AI technology like "agents" - essentially individual AI bots that conduct routine tasks somewhat autonomously could take over certain jobs.Clark said people who are more creative and have more, better ideas may actually have an advantage over AI technology."There are open questions about whether AI systems can be truly creative… there is not really evidence for that yet", he added. "At Anthropic, we're now limited more by the ability to generate good ideas than the ability to do the engineering to turn those ideas into reality."Nevertheless, Clark suggested a young person who may be feeling that an economy built on AI does not have a place for them should "develop a hobby" and pursue a liberal arts education."People that are creative and can think broadly, people that read a lot, people that have interests are the ones most benefited by this," Clark said. "Indulge in curiosity and it pays back in how you can use this technology."
contentParagraphs.zh: Anthropic 联合创始人费萨尔·伊斯兰 (Faisal Islam) 经济编辑和卡利·海斯 (Kali Hays) 技术记者卡利·海斯 (Kali Hays) 表示，我们需要阻止人工智能在没有人类参与的情况下发展。Anthropic 联合创始人杰克·克拉克 (Jack Clark) 呼吁有能力减缓人工智能 (AI) 的发展，并警告说，该技术已接近无需人类投入即可发展的地步。 “现在，人工智能行业就像有油门踏板，但没有刹车踏板。”他强调，人们需要通过政府政策来控制人工智能系统，这些系统只会变得更强大，对社会产生更广泛的影响。“世界需要进行一些思考，我们需要最终制定一些新的法规，让我们对这些系统充满信心。”他说。Anthropic 广受欢迎的聊天机器人 Claude 已经在运行其 80% 系统编写的代码。本身。克拉克表示，两年内实现 100% 的目标是有可能的，并且“将产生巨大的影响”。克拉克没有概述如何为人工智能研发创造“刹车踏板”，而是将人工智能与上世纪之交的石油繁荣和大亨进行了类比。“社会的反应是提出一个明智的政策和监管框架，让人们对石油以及石油可以为世界带来的好处充满信心，这意味着你不必担心领导公司的人的个性”，克拉克说。 “这显然就是我们最终的结局。” 然而，Anthropic 本周对美国总统唐纳德·特朗普颁布的一项关于人工智能的行政命令表示欢迎，该命令在对公司的指示中相对不干涉。追求技术进步的主要人工智能公司，包括 Anthropic、OpenAI 和谷歌，也没有表示将暂停自己的研究。这将成为新兴人工智能公司的首次公开上市之一，也是历史上最有价值的股票上市之一，因为私人投资者估计 Anthropic 的估值接近 1 万亿美元（7450 亿英镑）。克拉克表示，Anthropic 公开讨论人工智能技术不断增长的能力的动机并不是为了通过支付费用来进一步提升其声誉。他只是想“告诉世界我们用这种不寻常的技术在这些公司内部看到了什么”。 彭博社（盖蒂图片社）杰克·克拉克是 2021 年共同创立 Anthropic 的七名前 OpenAI 员工之一。自首席执行官达里奥·阿莫迪、克拉克和其他几位高管创立 Anthropic 以来，Anthropic 一直将自己定位为直言不讳地谈论人工智能带来的潜在风险。它甚至与美国国防部发生了公开争端，担心其人工智能工具将被用于对美国人的大规模监视和自主战争。“如果我们作为一个社会不就人工智能持续进步的影响进行认真的对话，我会为我的孩子担心，”克拉克告诉《新闻之夜》。 “这有潜在的巨大好处，但也存在风险。”克拉克补充说，风险之一是对经济的破坏，担心像“代理”这样的人工智能技术——本质上是独立执行日常任务的人工智能机器人，可能会接管某些工作。克拉克说，更有创造力、拥有更多、更好想法的人实际上可能比人工智能技术更具优势。“关于人工智能系统是否能够真正具有创造力，还存在一些悬而未决的问题……目前还没有真正的证据”，他补充道。 “在 Anthropic，我们现在更多地受到产生好想法的能力的限制，而不是通过工程将这些想法变成现实的能力。”尽管如此，克拉克建议，那些可能觉得人工智能经济不适合他们的年轻人应该“培养一种爱好”并追求文科教育。“有创造力、能广泛思考的人、读了很多书的人、有兴趣的人是从中受益最多的人，”克拉克说。 “沉浸在好奇心中，它会为你如何使用这项技术带来回报。”

articles.title: Scientists probe how pigeons use magnetism to navigate
articles.summary: Homing pigeons rely on a variety of signals to navigate, including magnetism. But it hasn't been clear how they detect magnetic cues. Researchers propose the answer may be found in the birds' livers.
articles.titleZh: 科学家探究鸽子如何利用磁力进行导航
articles.summaryZh: 信鸽依靠各种信号来导航，包括磁力。但目前尚不清楚它们如何检测磁线索。研究人员提出答案可能在鸟类的肝脏中找到。

contentParagraphs.en: Homing pigeons rely on a variety of signals to navigate, including magnetism. But it hasn't been clear how they detect magnetic cues. Researchers propose the answer may be found in the birds' livers.
contentParagraphs.zh: 信鸽依靠各种信号来导航，包括磁力。但目前尚不清楚它们如何检测磁线索。研究人员提出答案可能在鸟类的肝脏中找到。

contentParagraphs.en: SCOTT DETROW, HOST: Homing pigeons rely on a variety of signals to navigate the skies, including magnetism. But it's never been quite clear how exactly the birds detect magnetic cues. A team of researchers proposes the answer might be found in a pretty unexpected place. Here's science reporter Ari Daniel.ARI DANIEL: Drop a homing pigeon somewhere it's never been, and the bird can flap its way back home.MARTIN WIKELSKI: You have to provide them actually a good home so that they want to return, and that means good food and nice, cozy housing.DANIEL: To fly home, a pigeon has to figure out where it is and in which direction to go, says Martin Wikelski, who directs the Max Planck Institute of Animal Behavior in Germany. They rely on odor cues, the sun, visual landmarks and magnetic direction, which is using Earth's magnetic field to orient and navigate.WIKELSKI: It would be as if you were put into a forest where you know my home is east. And then you have a compass. You just go east. You end up at your cabin.DANIEL: Pigeons rely on magnetic direction under certain circumstances.WIKELSKI: That is, either at night or during completely overcast conditions.DANIEL: For decades, researchers have struggled to explain how these pigeons sense magnetic direction. Then some years back, Wikelski was attending an interdisciplinary meeting where he met someone he might never have encountered otherwise - immunologist Christian Kurts from the University of Bonn in Germany.CHRISTIAN KURTS: And in the coffee break, we talked to each other. Show me one scientist who doesn't like talking about their research (laughter).DANIEL: Kurts told him he'd been studying immune cells called macrophages when he stumbled upon something surprising. In the spleens of mice, these cells responded to a magnetic field.KURTS: One of the jobs of these cells is to eat and degrade old red blood cells. Our red blood cells contain hemoglobin.DANIEL: And hemoglobin contains iron. This bundle of iron atoms gives those immune cells magnetic properties, and the researchers wondered if something similar might be true for homing pigeons. So they examined cells from across the bird.CLIVIA LISOWSKI: And then we figured out that it's actually the liver who has the strongest magnetic properties.DANIEL: Clivia Lisowski is a cell biologist at the University of Bonn. Turns out the same kind of macrophages in the livers of homing pigeons also store oodles of iron atoms.LISOWSKI: And then the next question was, do they really play a role in animal navigation?DANIEL: To answer that, the researchers traveled to the south of Germany, near the Swiss border, with 34 pigeons that would need to fly 12 miles to reach home. In a little more than half the birds, the team used a drug to temporarily deplete those iron-containing macrophages when it was completely overcast, so no directional cues from the sun, says Martin Wikelski.WIKELSKI: That's the time when the pigeons have to rely upon their magnetic sense to find the direction. And the ones without the macrophages were basically lost.DANIEL: But the ones with the macrophages could do it. And when the sun came out, the birds that had been lost managed to find their way home.WIKELSKI: And then I thought, well, my God, this could really be real.DANIEL: In particular, these macrophages may be acting as a kind of internal compass that can detect magnetic direction. The findings are published in the journal Science.CATHERINE LOHMANN: I haven't yet seen the smoking gun that says this is the way they do it.DANIEL: Catherine Lohmann is a behavioral biologist at UNC-Chapel Hill who wasn't involved in the study. The authors do present microscope images showing the immune cells sitting alongside nerve fibers, but Lohmann isn't sure how a magnetic signal would then be relayed to the bird brain.LOHMANN: There's no mechanism that I know of for macrophages communicating with the nervous system.DANIEL: Still, she's open to the possibility. And the researchers are looking forward to the experiments needed to really clinch their new theory.KURTS: One thing I have learned in many years of research - you should never be too certain that you are right. Nature's always more complicated than you think.DANIEL: For NPR News, I'm Ari Daniel.(SOUNDBITE OF THEE SACRED SOULS SONG, "EASIER SAID THAN DONE")
contentParagraphs.zh: 主持人斯科特·德特罗：信鸽依靠各种信号在天空中导航，包括磁力。但人们一直不太清楚鸟类到底是如何检测到磁信号的。一组研究人员提出，答案可能会在一个意想不到的地方找到。科学记者阿里·丹尼尔 (Ari Daniel) 报道。 阿里·丹尼尔 (ARI DANIEL)：把一只信鸽扔到它从未去过的地方，它就能拍打着翅膀飞回家。 马丁·维克尔斯基 (Martin WIKELSKI)：你必须为它们提供一个真正好的家，这样它们才会愿意返回，这意味着美味的食物和漂亮、舒适的住房。丹尼尔：马克斯·普朗克动物行为研究所所长马丁·维克尔斯基 (Martin Wikelski) 说，要飞回家，鸽子必须弄清楚它在哪里以及要朝哪个方向飞。德国。它们依靠气味线索、太阳、视觉地标和磁力方向，即利用地球磁场来定向和导航。 WIKELSKI：这就好像你被放进一片森林，你知道我的家在东方。然后你就有了一个指南针。你只要向东走就可以了。你最终会到达你的小屋。 丹尼尔： 在某些情况下，鸽子依赖磁力方向。 WIKELSKI： 也就是说，无论是在夜间还是在完全阴天的条件下。 丹尼尔： 几十年来，研究人员一直在努力解释这些鸽子如何感知磁力方向。几年前，Wikelski 参加一个跨学科会议，在那里他遇到了一个他可能永远不会遇到的人 - 来自德国波恩大学的免疫学家 Christian Kurts。 CHRISTIAN Kurts：在茶歇时，我们互相交谈。让我看看一位不喜欢谈论他们的研究的科学家（笑声）。 丹尼尔：科茨告诉他，当他偶然发现一些令人惊讶的东西时，他一直在研究称为巨噬细胞的免疫细胞。在小鼠的脾脏中，这些细胞对磁场做出反应。库尔茨：这些细胞的工作之一是吃掉并降解旧的红细胞。我们的红细胞含有血红蛋白。 丹尼尔：血红蛋白含有铁。这束铁原子赋予这些免疫细胞磁性，研究人员想知道信鸽是否也有类似的情况。所以他们检查了鸟身上的细胞。 CLIVIA LISOWSKI：然后我们发现实际上是肝脏具有最强的磁性。 DANIEL：Clivia Lisowski 是波恩大学的细胞生物学家。事实证明，信鸽肝脏中的同类巨噬细胞也储存了大量铁原子。 LISOWSKI：接下来的问题是，它们真的在动物导航中发挥作用吗？ 丹尼尔：为了回答这个问题，研究人员前往德国南部靠近瑞士边境的地方，带着 34 只鸽子，它们需要飞行 12 英里才能回家。马丁·威克尔斯基 (Martin Wikelski) 表示，研究小组在天气完全阴天时，对一半多一点的鸽子使用了一种药物来暂时耗尽这些含铁巨噬细胞，因此没有来自太阳的方向提示。威克尔斯基：那时鸽子必须依靠磁感来寻找方向。没有巨噬细胞的基本上都消失了。 丹尼尔：但是有巨噬细胞的可以做到。当太阳出来时，迷路的鸟儿设法找到了回家的路。 WIKELSKI：然后我想，好吧，天啊，这可能真的是真的。 DANIEL：特别是，这些巨噬细胞可能充当一种内部指南针，可以检测磁方向。研究结果发表在《科学》杂志上。 凯瑟琳·洛曼：我还没有看到确凿的证据表明他们就是这样做的。丹尼尔：凯瑟琳·洛曼是北卡罗来纳大学教堂山分校的行为生物学家，她没有参与这项研究。作者确实提供了显微镜图像，显示免疫细胞位于神经纤维旁边，但洛曼不确定磁信号如何传递到鸟的大脑。洛曼：据我所知，巨噬细胞与神经系统通讯的机制尚不明确。丹尼尔：不过，她对这种可能性持开放态度。研究人员期待着真正证实他们的新理论所需的实验。 库尔茨：我在多年的研究中学到了一件事——你永远不应该太确定自己是对的。大自然总是比你想象的更复杂。丹尼尔：NPR 新闻，我是阿里·丹尼尔。（《神圣灵魂之歌》原声片段，“说起来容易做起来难”）

articles.title: How the world failed a mother’s children, killed in Israeli strikes on Gaza
articles.summary: How the world failed a mother’s children, killed in Israeli strikes on Gaza
articles.titleZh: 世界如何辜负了在以色列对加沙的袭击中丧生的母亲的孩子
articles.summaryZh: 世界如何辜负了在以色列对加沙的袭击中丧生的母亲的孩子

contentParagraphs.en: NewsFeedPalestinian journalist and mother Aya Shamaa wrote about how an Israeli strike killed her children, newborn Ryan and seven-year-old Yaman. articles.title: New claimants seek to sue Elon Musk’s xAI after Labour MP’s test case
articles.summary:
Jess Asato’s lawyer says others want to take action over demeaning sexualised material created by Grok AI tool
New claimants have come forward to take legal action against Elon Musk’s company xAI after the Labour MP Jess Asato launched a test case against the firm over demeaning
articles.titleZh: 在工党议员的测试案例后，新索赔人寻求起诉埃隆·马斯克的 xAI
articles.summaryZh:
Jess Asato 的律师表示，其他人希望对 Grok AI 工具创建的贬低性内容采取行动
在工党议员杰西·阿萨托（Jess Asato）针对埃隆·马斯克（Elon Musk）的公司 xAI 发起侮辱性测试案件后，新的索赔人站出来对该公司采取法律行动

contentParagraphs.en: New claimants have come forward to take legal action against Elon Musk’s company xAI after the Labour MP Jess Asato launched a test case against the firm over demeaning sexualised material created by its Grok AI tool.A handful of complainants contacted Asato’s lawyer on Thursday in response to coverage of the MP’s decision to sue Musk’s company for damages over its creation and circulation of fake images of her in a bikini and an AI-created video that she said showed her “being chloroformed and prepared for a sexual assault”.Ravi Naik, the legal director of the law firm AWO, said he was already acting for “multiple individuals” hoping to take action against Musk’s company over degrading, non-consensual content generated by Grok. Many of the claimants had struggled to persuade X to remove the images until they received legal support, he said.“This is the test case on liability for AI developers. Just as if you’re an architect and build a building, you have liability for that architecture,” Naik said of the claim he has lodged on Asato’s behalf at the high court in London. “Those that build and deploy AI models make design choices about how these models operate. This will be the case that looks at liability for decisions in those design choices.”The claim argues xAI violated data protection law and breached Asato’s private information when it allowed the images to be generated.A bikinification trend went viral on Musk’s platform in January when Grok generated about 3m sexualised images in less than two weeks, according to researchers who said it “became an industrial-scale machine for the production of sexual abuse material”. The AI tool allowed users to alter online images of real people with requests such as “put her in a bikini” or “remove her clothes”.Musk’s company later put the technology behind a paywall and limited the chatbot’s capacity to fulfil users’ prompts to create sexualised images.Asato said she wanted the legal action to demonstrate that “AI companies are responsible for the design choices that they make when they launch their products”.She said: “There were guardrails that the engineers and Elon Musk could have put in place to stop Grok from being able to create sexualised images but they decided not to put those guardrails in place. I’m hoping that my legal action will help to rein in tech companies and remind them that they cannot act with impunity.”She said she found the experience of seeing fake non-consensual stripped images of herself “psychologically distressing”. “This goes to the core of understanding what it means not to consent to something which literally strips your clothes off and makes you vulnerable,” she said.When she complained about the harm caused by the Grok trend in January, she received a stream of abusive responses from commentators on X. One of those was shared by Musk, and a user posted the AI-generated video of her apparently being sedated with chloroform in response to his retweet.“Musk actually amplified the hatred against me, which then led to the video that really was horrific,” she said. “He could have made different choices about the way he and his company approached the fact that I, as an elected politician in the UK, was saying that I felt humiliated and distressed by what his product was doing.”On Thursday, Asato received further verbal abuse on X in response to her announcement of the legal proceedings, including a new AI-generated image of her stripped to a bikini, created using a different tool.Keir Starmer said Asato was “absolutely right” to take legal action against xAI over the “disgusting” images created of her.The legal action comes amid heightened sensitivity to Musk’s involvement in UK domestic affairs, after a flurry of posts from the billionaire commenting on the police response to the murder of Henry Nowak.Peter Kyle, the business secretary and a former technology secretary, said it was important that UK politicians were “assertive” in holding Musk to account for the content on his platforms, noting that Musk was “taking a much more active and extreme role in British politics”.“Musk is a complex and extreme person. He’s an extremely successful innovator and commercialiser of innovation, but he also has extreme personal views,” Kyle said.xAI did not respond to a request for comment.Additional reporting by Jessica Elgot
contentParagraphs.zh: 工党议员杰西·阿萨托 (Jess Asato) 就该公司的 Grok 人工智能工具创建的贬低色情内容发起了一项针对该公司的测试案件，新的索赔人已对埃隆·马斯克 (Elon Musk) 的公司 xAI 采取法律行动。 AWO 律师事务所的法律总监拉维·奈克 (Ravi Naik) 表示，他已经在代表“多个人”，希望就 Grok 生成的侮辱性、未经同意的内容对马斯克的公司采取行动。他说，许多索赔人一直在努力说服 X 删除这些图像，直到获得法律支持。“这是对人工智能开发人员责任的测试案例。就像你是一名建筑师，建造了一栋大楼，你对这座建筑负有责任一样，”奈克在谈到他代表 Asato 向伦敦高等法院提出的索赔时说道。 “那些构建和部署人工智能模型的人对这些模型的运作方式做出了设计选择。在这种情况下，我们将审视这些设计选择中的决策责任。”该索赔认为 xAI 违反了数据保护法，并在允许生成图像时侵犯了 Asato 的私人信息。一月份，当 Grok 在不到两周的时间内生成了约 300 万张性感图像时，比基尼化趋势在马斯克的平台上疯传，研究人员表示，它“成为了生产性虐待的工业规模机器”材料”。该人工智能工具允许用户更改真人的在线图像，提出“让她穿上比基尼”或“脱掉她的衣服”等请求。马斯克的公司后来将该技术置于付费墙后面，并限制了聊天机器人满足用户创建性感图像的提示的能力。安里说，她希望采取法律行动来证明“人工智能公司对他们在推出产品时做出的设计选择负责”。她说：“工程师和埃隆·马斯克本可以设置一些护栏阻止 Grok 制作色情图像，但他们决定不设置这些护栏，我希望我的法律行动将有助于控制科技公司，并提醒他们，他们的行为不能不受惩罚。”她说，看到未经同意的假冒自己的剥离图像的经历“心理上令人痛苦”。她说：“这涉及到理解不同意那些真正剥光你的衣服、让你变得脆弱的事情意味着什么的核心。”当她在一月份抱怨 Grok 趋势造成的伤害时，她收到了来自 X 评论员的一系列辱骂回应。其中一条是马斯克分享的，一名用户发布了人工智能生成的视频，视频中她显然是在接受氯仿镇静剂以回应他的转发。“马斯克实际上放大了对我的仇恨，这导致了视频真的很可怕，”她说。 “他本可以对他和他的公司处理这一事实做出不同的选择，我作为一名英国民选政客，说我对他的产品所做的事情感到羞辱和痛苦。”周四，在 X 宣布法律诉讼后，Asato 受到了进一步的谩骂，其中包括一张由 AI 生成的她脱光比基尼的新图像，该图像是使用不同的工具创建的。Keir Starmer 表示，Asato 对 xAI 采取法律行动“绝对正确”。她的形象“令人作呕”。在这位亿万富翁发表一系列帖子评论警方对亨利·诺瓦克谋杀案的反应后，人们对马斯克参与英国内政的敏感度日益提高。英国商务大臣兼前技术大臣彼得·凯尔表示，英国政界人士“坚决”要求马斯克对其平台上的内容负责，这一点很重要，并指出马斯克“在英国政治中扮演着更加积极和极端的角色”。凯尔说：“他是一位非常成功的创新者和创新商业化者，但他也有极端的个人观点。”xAI 没有回应置评请求。杰西卡·埃尔戈特的补充报道。

articles.title: Failing sea defences 'disaster' for nature reserve
articles.summary: An internationally important nature reserve in Hampshire is under threat from failing flood defences.
articles.titleZh: 失败的海防给自然保护区带来“灾难”
articles.summaryZh: 汉普郡的一个国际重要自然保护区正因防洪失败而受到威胁。

contentParagraphs.en: Failing sea defences 'disaster' for nature reserve1 day agoJon CuthillSouth of England environment correspondentBBCFarlington Marshes near Portsmouth is one of the oldest nature reserves managed by Hampshire and Isle of Wight Wildlife TrustAn internationally-important nature reserve faces "disaster" after being flooded by seawater because of failing coastal defences, conservationists have said.The 120-hectare (300-acre) Farlington Marshes Nature Reserve near Portsmouth is designated as a Special Protection Area (SPA), Special Area for Conservation (SAC) and Ramsar site.Hampshire and Isle of Wight Wildlife Trust has highlighted a failing tidal valve and crumbling sea wall which are letting salt water flood grazing marshland and wash away birds' nests and reed beds.The Environment Agency (EA) said it was working to find a permanent fix but estimated it would cost about £90m to replace the entire 2-mile (3.5km) sea defence.The site is home to bird species including bearded tit, avocet, redshank and lapwing.Jamie Marsh, director of land management for Hampshire and Isle of Wight Wildlife Trust, said: "This is a bit of a disaster", with eight hectares of reed bed already lost."We have a tidal flap that's not sealing off properly and we're seeing water flooding into the site. "The elevated sea levels have flooded out a lot of these areas and consequently flooded out a lot of the nesting areas. So nests have been lost."Birds have been forced to move to higher ground to renest and rebreed.The tidal flap first broke in the spring of 2024. A temporary repair was put in place by the EA which is responsible for the sea defences.Earlier this year, engineers installed a permanent replacement valve but that too has since failed.Marsh said it had left the situation "back to square one"."Habitats that were recovering are now back under threat and under pressure again."Areas of salt marsh and marsh grazing like Farlington are incredibly important and rare habitat in the south of England.Not only does it provide a unique home for wildlife but coastal fringes act as both a natural flood defence and a carbon sink.But with rising sea levels due to climate change such sites are getting squeezed out.Development and hard infrastructure - in Farlington's case, the A27 - means there is nowhere for the marshes to retreat inland. In the future, unless action is taken, there is a real risk they will be lost altogether.But finding areas of land to recreate lost salt marsh on a large scale is a near impossible task on an already crowded coast line.Dr Stefanie Carter, coastal eco systems scientist at the UK Centre for Ecology and Hydrology, described it as a "declining habitat"."It is possible to create salt marshes and restore some of the marsh we've lost but it probably won't be possible to do this at the scale.""It's similar to peatland where in the past they've been regarded as wastelands. In the past they were dried out for agriculture and now they're realising peatlands are really important.""And it's the same for saltmarshes. We're realising how important they are so we're trying to prioritise restoring them."Contractors have returned to the site and installed another temporary fix to try to stop more sea water entering the nature reserveBack at Farlington Marshes Nature Reserve, the EA has put in another temporary fix to stop sea water overwhelming the site.More changes to the water control unit are due to be made later this month.A statement said: "These sea wall assets are nearing the end of their operational life, and we have spent hundreds of thousands of pounds and officer time in recent years to keep them working until we can find a longer term solution."We fully recognise the importance of Farlington Marshes to the community in terms of flood protection, recreation and as a wildlife haven, and remain committed to resolve the issue as quickly as possible."More on this storyRelated internet links
contentParagraphs.zh: 海洋防御失败给自然保护区带来“灾难” 1天前 英格兰南部环境通讯员 BBC 朴茨茅斯附近的法灵顿沼泽自然保护区是汉普郡和怀特岛野生动物信托基金管理的最古老的自然保护区之一。自然资源保护主义者表示，由于海岸防御失败，国际重要的自然保护区在被海水淹没后面临“灾难”。 朴茨茅斯附近占地 120 公顷（300 英亩）的法灵顿沼泽自然保护区朴茨茅斯被指定为特别保护区 (SPA)、特别保护区 (SAC) 和拉姆萨尔湿地。汉普郡和怀特岛野生动物信托基金强调了失效的潮汐阀和摇摇欲坠的海堤，导致咸水淹没沼泽地并冲走鸟巢和芦苇床。环境署 (EA) 表示正在努力寻找永久解决方案，但估计需要花费约 9000 万英镑来更换整个 2 英里（3.5 公里）海域该地点是髯山雀、反嘴鹬、红脚鹬和田凫等鸟类的家园。汉普郡和怀特岛野生动物信托基金土地管理主任杰米·马什说：“这有点灾难”，八公顷的芦苇床已经消失。结果淹没了许多筑巢区。因此，巢穴已经消失。“鸟类被迫迁移到地势较高的地方重新栖息和繁殖。潮汐瓣在 2024 年春天首次破裂。负责海上防御的 EA 进行了临时修复。今年早些时候，工程师安装了一个永久性的替换阀门，但此后也失败了。马什表示，这使情况“回到原点”。“正在恢复的栖息地现在又面临威胁和压力。 “像法灵顿这样的盐沼和沼泽放牧地区是英格兰南部极其重要和稀有的栖息地。它不仅为野生动物提供了独特的家园，而且沿海边缘既是天然的防洪设施，又是碳汇。但随着气候变化导致海平面上升，这些地点正在被挤出。发展和硬基础设施 - 就法灵顿的情况而言，A27 - 意味着沼泽无处可撤退到内陆。在未来，除非采取行动，但在已经拥挤的海岸线上，找到大规模的土地来重建消失的盐沼几乎是一项不可能的任务。英国生态和水文学中心的沿海生态系统科学家斯蒂芬妮·卡特博士将其描述为“正在衰退的栖息地”。“创建盐沼并恢复我们失去的一些沼泽是可能的，但在大规模上可能不可能做到这一点。”“它类似于泥炭地。”过去它们被视为荒地。过去，它们因农业而干旱，现在他们意识到泥炭地非常重要。”“盐沼也是如此。我们意识到它们的重要性，因此我们正在努力优先恢复它们。“承包商已返回现场并安装了另一个临时修复装置，试图阻止更多的海水进入自然保护区。回到法灵顿沼泽自然保护区，环境署已安装了另一个临时修复装置，以阻止海水淹没该场地。本月晚些时候将对水控制装置进行更多更改。一份声明称：“这些海堤资产已接近其使用寿命，我们已花费了数十万英镑和官员“我们充分认识到法灵顿沼泽在防洪、娱乐和作为野生动物天堂方面对社区的重要性，并继续致力于尽快解决这个问题。”更多关于这个故事的相关互联网链接
